flashcards.back: A class that cannot be instantiated and contains at least one pure virtual function.

flashcards.front: How is a pure virtual function declared?
flashcards.back: Using the syntax: virtual returnType functionName(parameters) = 0;

flashcards.front: Can an abstract class have constructors?
flashcards.back: Yes, abstract classes can have constructors which are called when derived class objects are created.

flashcards.front: What happens if a derived class doesn't implement all pure virtual functions?
flashcards.back: The derived class remains abstract and cannot be instantiated.

flashcards.front: Why are virtual destructors important in abstract base classes?
flashcards.back: To ensure proper cleanup of derived class objects when deleted through base class pointers.

flashcards.front: Can abstract classes contain implemented methods?
flashcards.back: Yes, abstract classes can contain both pure virtual functions and regular virtual or non-virtual functions with implementations.

flashcards.front: What is the main purpose of abstract classes?
flashcards.back: To define common interfaces and enforce implementation of specific behaviors in derived classes.

flashcards.front: How do abstract classes support polymorphism?
flashcards.back: They allow base class pointers/references to point to derived class objects, enabling runtime polymorphism.
